Welcome to MammoCare Hub

Your one-stop destination for comprehensive breast health information, resources, and support. At MammoCare Hub, we are dedicated to empowering individuals with knowledge about breast health, early detection, and preventive care.

Our Mission

Our mission is to provide accurate, up-to-date information on breast health, promote awareness about breast cancer, and offer resources for those affected by breast health issues. We strive to create a supportive community where individuals can find guidance, share experiences, and access the latest advancements in breast health care.

What We Offer

📚 Educational Resources

  • Access a wealth of information on breast health, including articles, videos, and infographics covering topics such as breast anatomy, risk factors, screening methods, and preventive measures.

🤝 Support Community

  • Get exposed to various online communities to connect with others navigating breast health challenges. Share your experiences, ask questions, and find support from individuals who understand what you're going through.

Breast Cancer

What is Breast Cancer?

Breast Cancer is a disease characterized by the uncontrolled growth of abnormal cells in the breast tissue. Most breast cancers begin in the ducts (tubes that carry milk to the nipple) or lobules (glands that produce milk). It can spread outside the breast through blood and lymph vessels; when it spreads to other parts of the body, it is referred to as metastasized.

Symptoms
  • A new lump or mass in the breast or underarm
  • Thickening or swelling of part of the breast
  • Irritation or dimpling of the breast skin
  • Redness or flaky skin of the nipple area
  • Nipple abnormalities, such as inverted nipples, pain surrounding the nipple, or discharge other than breast milk including blood
  • Any pain or change in the size or shape of the breast
Breast Cancer Progression
All images originated from the “breast cancer tab” of https://smart.servier.com/

Risk Factors You Cannot Change

  • Genetic Factors
  • Family History or Genetic Mutations (BRCA1 & BRCA2)
  • Reproductive History
  • Dense Breasts
  • History of Noncancerous Breast Disease
  • Advanced Age or Gender
  • Non-Genetic Factors
  • Previous Exposure to Radiation
  • (DES) Diethylstilbestrol Exposure

Modifiable Risk Factors

  • Body Weight
  • Alcohol Consumption
  • Hormone Therapy
  • Physical Activity

Prevention, Early Detection, & Treatment

🛡️ Prevention Strategies

  • Modifiable Lifestyle Choices
  • Managing Hormonal Exposure
  • Chemoprevention
  • Prophylactic Surgery

🔍 Early Detection Methods

  • Mammography
  • Clinical Breast Examination (CBE) or Breast Self-Examination (BSE)
  • Ultrasound or Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI)
Mammography

Mammography is the procedure of receiving low-dose X-rays to image the breast to detect breast cancer, often identifying tumors or abnormalities before they can be felt. Women are suggested to get screened every 2 years beginning at 40 years of age.

Treatment Options
  • Surgery (Lumpectomy or Mastectomy)
  • Radiation Therapy
  • Chemotherapy
  • Hormone Therapy
  • Targeted & Biological Therapies
  • Clinical Trials

The Healthcare Industry & Social Media

Addressing Social Media

Social media is a powerful tool that spreads valuable information online in an instant which can spread rapidly through algorithms, viral trends, and user interactivity. With that in mind, the healthcare industry has adapted to the digital age by being provided the opportunity for health professionals to meet patients where they are, combat misinformation, and improve public health outreach.

With a majority of the world’s population having access to the internet, integrating social media and online communities in patient care yields numerous benefits, including improved patient care, health outcomes, and accessibility to healthcare information and educational resources (Anawade, P. A., 2024). The benefits of social media within healthcare include fostering a supportive community, building professional collaboration, and providing patients with the support they need wherever they may be.

Social media and digital platforms have emerged as promising tools to enhance breast cancer screening awareness and participation. Social media use correlates with increased awareness of cancer screening modalities (Mohd, Z., 2025). Furthermore, due to the rise of short video clips and vlogging, the everyday person is exposed to a plethora of influencers and content creators who narrate their lives on a routine basis. Many social media users develop parasocial relations with these influencers and could believe everything that they say without questioning their credentials — which is dangerous because social media is flooded with fact-based, opinion-based, or maliciously deceptive information.

Social Media Influence on Women’s Mammography Perceptions
Survey Introduction

With social media being prevalent in everyone's life, a survey was conducted that examined how social media influences women's perceptions, fears, and behaviors regarding breast cancer and preventative routine screenings like mammography. It targeted the intersection of health literacy, social media techniques, and medical decision-making. The survey consisted of 50 women ranging from the ages of 18–75 years of age.

Survey Results
60%
of women surveyed have gone in for a mammogram
66%
found social media an accessible source for mammography awareness
92%
were on social media (Facebook, X, YouTube, Instagram)
36%
saw a “Get Ready with Me” for routine medical appointments in the last month
58%
were influenced by certified health professional content creators
42%
had concerns about the discomfort of mammograms
26%
had doubts about professional advice on screening frequency
74%
were encouraged to seek more information on breast cancer
Survey Conclusion

The survey collected data on women’s perceptions, fears, and behaviors in regards to breast cancer and preventative routine screenings. With the examination of health literacy, social media techniques, and medical decision-making, this data showed the accessibility of receiving mammogram awareness and/or related procedures via social platform familiarity. It demonstrates how quickly information can spread and brings awareness to the topic of breast cancer and preventative screening.

Additionally, individuals with higher education or those who are already health-conscious are generally more likely to be conscious about maintaining a healthy lifestyle and are therefore encouraged or actively pursuing booking a mammogram appointment in the later future. Of the 74% of women encouraged to seek more information, 60% had a post-secondary education such as a bachelor’s or master’s degree. Also, younger women (under 35 years of age) tended to be more physically active and showed more interest in ensuring they get screened when they turn 40.

Medical Misinformation

What is Medical Misinformation?

Medical misinformation refers to false or misleading information related to health and medical topics. It can spread rapidly through social media, websites, and other platforms, leading to confusion and potentially harmful consequences for individuals seeking accurate health information.

How to Spot Medical Misinformation

🔎 Check the Source

  • Look for reputable sources such as government health agencies, academic institutions, and established medical organizations.
  • Be cautious of information from unknown or unverified sources, especially those that lack credentials or have a history of spreading misinformation.

⚠️ Beware of Sensationalism

  • Be skeptical of headlines or claims that seem exaggerated, fear-inducing, or too good to be true.
  • Look for balanced reporting that presents evidence-based information rather than relying on emotional appeals.

🩺 Consult Healthcare Professionals

  • Always consult with qualified healthcare professionals for personalized medical advice and information.
  • Be cautious of self-diagnosis or relying solely on online information without professional guidance.

Common Misconceptions

Common Misconceptions About Breast Cancer
MythReality
Breast cancer only affects women.Breast cancer impacts both women and men.
A lump is the only symptom of breast cancer.There are various symptoms that can indicate breast cancer, such as nipple discharge, changes in breast size or shape, and skin changes.
Mammograms cause breast cancer.Mammograms use low-dose radiation and are considered safe for early detection of breast cancer.
Underwire bras cause breast cancer.There is no scientific evidence to support this claim.
Breast cancer is always contagious.Breast cancer cannot be spread from person to person.
Breast cancer is uniform.Breast cancer can vary significantly in terms of its characteristics, behavior, and response to treatment.
Common Misconceptions Regarding Mammogram Screenings
MythReality
Mammograms spread cancer.Mammograms use low-dose radiation and are considered safe for early detection of breast cancer.
Mammograms are 100% accurate.Mammograms can miss some cancers, and additional imaging may be needed. Also, it could lead to overdiagnosis or overtreatment.
Self-exams are sufficient for detecting breast cancer.While self-exams can be helpful, they should not replace professional screenings.
